data.title: 10 of the best Asian time travel movies to watch after Hong Kong hit Back to the Past
data.description:
Hong Kong movie star and producer Louis Koo Tin-lok is currently rewriting history – and box office records – with the time-travel adventure Back to the Past.
Taking in over HK$60 million (US$7.7 million) since its New Year’s Eve debut, the film is a much-anticipated sequel to 2001’s A Step into the Past, a favourite television series from Hong Kong broadcaster TVB. It sends Koo’s security agent hurtling back to the Qin dynasty, reuniting him with many original cast members, including Raymond Lam Fung and Jessica Hsuan.
The film strikes a lucrative balance between high-concept sci-fi and warm nostalgia – cementing a winning streak for Koo following the massive box office success of Warriors of Future (2022) and Twilight of the Warriors: Walled In (2024).
While many recent time travel films have used time loops or communication between divergent timelines, Back to the Past revels in the old-school charm of following its fish-out-of-water protagonists.
In celebration of the film’s success, here are 10 other examples of big-screen time-travel escapism from across Asia that are well worth your attention.
1. G.I. Samurai (1979)


In a scenario not entirely dissimilar to the events of Back to the Past, Kosei Saito’s 1979 action blockbuster sees a fully armed squadron of JSDF forces transported back in time 400 years, where they fight alongside the legendary feudal lord Nagao Kagetora.
Japanese action legend Sonny Chiba stars as the leader of the displaced soldiers, who slowly come to the realisation that their hi-tech artillery is no match for shrewd strategic leadership and the honourable Bushido code.
The film was remade in 2005 as Samurai Commando: Mission 1549.
2. Aditya 369 (1991)


Touted as Indian cinema’s first time-travel movie, Singeetam Srinivasa Rao’s Telugu-language epic opens with a young boy (Tarun) witnessing the theft of a priceless 16th-century diamond.
He becomes part of a motley crew, led by Nandamuri Balakrishna’s handsome rogue, that is accidentally transported back in time 500 years to the court of Emperor Sri Krishnadevaraya (also played by Balakrishna).
What follows is a typically chaotic, riotously entertaining collage of action, comedy and romance that even includes a sojourn into the far-flung future, where humanity cowers underground following a nuclear war.
3. God of Gamblers III: Back to Shanghai (1991)


Stephen Chow Sing-chi’s “Saint of Gamblers” Sing-cho is magically transported back to 1930s Shanghai in this action-packed comedy caper from Wong Jing.
There, he teams up with his own grandfather (Ng Man-tat) to beat a formidable French gambler, fend off Japanese forces, and come to the aid of Ray Lui Leung-wai’s charismatic millionaire.
Amusingly, Lui’s character, Ding Lik, is the same one he played in the popular 1980s TVB series The Bund, opposite original God of Gamblers star Chow Yun-fat.
4. He Ain’t Heavy, He’s My Father (1993)


The two Tony Leungs, Ka-fai and Chiu-wai, play father and son in this thoughtfully executed fantasy drama by Peter Chan Ho-sun and Lee Chi-ngai, who lifted a page or two from Back to the Future’s guide to manipulating the past.
Leung Chiu-wai plays the unappreciative son, Yuen, who bemoans the big heart of his father (Leung Ka-fai) and his poor business sense until tragedy strikes and Yuen is transported back in time, where he must play cupid to his mismatched parents-to-be.
In 2008, TVB remade the film as the series A Chip Off the Old Block, starring Ron Ng Cheuk-hei and Sunny Chan Kam-hung.
5. 2009: Lost Memories (2002)


Instead of scrambling to prevent time travel from changing future events, Lee Si-myung’s ambitious action thriller exists in a world where the damage has already been done.
Jang Dong-gun plays an ethnically Korean special agent in a near future, where Japan has annexed the Korean peninsula and has risen to become a global superpower.
Upon discovering that his reality is the result of a manipulated timeline, he sets out to find the ancient artefact responsible and restore the last century of his nation’s history.
6. Secret (2007)


Written and directed by its star, Taiwanese pop sensation Jay Chou Jie-lun, this unabashedly romantic drama tells the story of a magical love affair between high-schooler Xiang-lun (Chou) and the mysterious Xiao-yu (Gwei Lun-mei).
Central to their courtship is a piece of old sheet music that wields the power to transport people back and forth through time, should it be played on a specific antique piano.
Anthony Wong Chau-sang also features in this tear-jerker, which was tailored to strike a chord with lovestruck teens rather than sci-fi fans.
7. A Boy and His Samurai (2010)


Decidedly less action-packed than many time travel adventures, Yoshihiro Nakamura’s delightfully understated comedy follows a ubiquitous theme in Japanese cinema: lamenting homespun traditions in today’s fast-paced urban world.
When a young warrior (Ryo Nishikido) from Japan’s Edo period arrives unexpectedly in the present day, he befriends a beautiful divorcee (Rie Tomosaka) and her young son.
Over time, the samurai develops a fascination for contemporary desserts and turns his hand to becoming a pastry chef in this heartwarming adaptation of Gen Araki’s popular manga.
8. Beyond the Infinite Two Minutes (2020)


Proving that successful science fiction does not demand a blockbuster budget, Junta Yamaguchi stages a wildly inventive single-take comedy, in which a hapless cafe owner (Kazunari Tosa) discovers that his computer screen displays a live feed from precisely two minutes into the future.
From here, the film explores a series of mind-bending possibilities as the characters attempt to manipulate the course of history, leading to a frenzied comedy of errors more intelligent and insightful than anything the genre has conjured up before or since.
9. Hi, Mom (2021)


Chinese writer-director-star Jia Ling adapted her own 2016 television series into one of the most successful hits of all time at mainland China’s box office.
In a premise that riffs on Back to the Future, Jia plays a layabout teenager in 2001 who is magicked back in time 20 years, befriending her own mother (Zhang Xiaofei), who is her age and working in a factory.
Revelling in nostalgia for this period of sweeping economic reform, Jia spins a hilarious yet heartfelt tale of regret and redemption that packs a surprisingly emotional punch.
10. Alienoid (2022) and Alienoid: Return to the Future (2024)


In Choi Dong-hoon’s hugely ambitious two-part saga, the Earth is being used unwittingly as a penal colony for alien criminals, both in the present day and in Korea’s 14th-century Goryeo era.
Featuring a star-studded cast, led by Ryu Jun-yeol, Kim Tae-ri and Kim Woo-bin, the plot sees heroes and villains travel back and forth between the two periods in their efforts to conquer the Earth or save it from extraterrestrial invasion.
Less than the sum of its convoluted parts, perhaps, but very entertaining nonetheless.
Want more articles like this? Follow SCMP Film on Facebook

data.title: Philippines offers 2-week visa-free stays to lure Chinese tourists
data.description:
Chinese nationals will be allowed to enter the Philippines without a visa for up to two weeks from Friday, according to the Department of Foreign Affairs in Manila.
The visa-waiver programme will cover Chinese nationals entering the Philippines for both tourism and business, but will apply only to those entering via Ninoy Aquino International Airport in Metro Manila and Mactan-Cebu International Airport in Cebu.
“This is in line with the president’s directive to facilitate trade, investments and tourism, as well as strengthen people-to-people exchanges between the Philippines and China,” the department said on Thursday.
The stay will not be extendable nor convertible to any other type of visa. The visa-free policy is set to last one year, pending a review before its expiration in 2027.
Before Thursday’s announcement, Chinese nationals could stay for up to seven days in the Philippines without a visa if they had a return ticket and valid visas to Australia, the United States, Canada, Japan or the Schengen Area.


Tourism is a major economic driver in the Philippines, but the country is struggling to attract international visitors in pre-pandemic numbers.
Chinese visitors were being deterred by visa disruptions, security fears and limited flights, which were now operating at only about 45 per cent of the pre-pandemic levels, the Philippine Department of Tourism said last month.
Last year, the Philippines had 5.6 million international arrivals by December 20, short of the 5.9 million it reported in 2024, according to the Philippine Information Agency.
China accounted for 262,144 of the arrivals in 2025, putting the country sixth as a source of tourists behind South Korea, the US, Japan, Australia and Canada.
The administration of President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr has pledged to streamline visa processes and make the country more accessible to tourists, particularly to those from China, one of the world’s largest outbound tourism markets.
In November, the Philippines reintroduced an electronic visa (e-Visa) system as part of an effort to restore confidence and ease travel for Chinese tourists.
“The e-Visa resumption is a critical step forward and a clear signal that the Philippines is open, ready and eager to welcome our Chinese friends,” Ireneo Reyes, Philippine tourism attache to China, said in December.
However, it is not clear how much of a lure the visa-free policy will be for Chinese travellers, given persistent tensions in the South China Sea between the two countries.
There have also been concerns in the Philippines that some Chinese visitors have been involved in scams and illegal gambling.
According to a survey by Tsinghua University’s Centre for International Security and Strategy, Chinese public opinion of the Philippines was the third lowest among China’s 14 neighbouring countries, just ahead of Myanmar and India.

data.title: Death toll from Hong Kong fire rises to 168 after more remains identified
data.description:
The death toll of Hong Kong’s worst fire in decades has risen to 168 from 161 after the remains of more victims were identified, the city’s security chief has said.
Secretary for Security Chris Tang Ping-keung updated the figures for the Wang Fuk Court inferno in Tai Po, saying that all the victims had now been identified following dental examinations and DNA testing of remains.
“We have notified the families of all 168 victims, and they have since confirmed the identities of the deceased,” Tang said at Hong Kong airport on Thursday after returning from a visit to Beijing.
“All bodies and remains have now been identified, leaving no unidentified remains. We also have clarified the status of every individual previously reported as missing or unreachable. Consequently, the figure of 168 fatalities is final.”
Among the 168 victims of the November 26 blaze were a firefighter who died in the line of duty, two renovation workers, five construction workers and 10 domestic helpers, he said.
More to follow …

data.title: Hong Kong watchdog warns against using Musk’s Grok AI bot to create indecent images
data.description:
Hong Kong’s privacy watchdog has raised concerns over the potential misuse of the artificial intelligence (AI) chatbot Grok, developed by Elon Musk’s company, warning that using its image-generation function to create indecent or malicious content could amount to criminal offences.
The Office of the Privacy Commissioner for Personal Data (PCPD) issued a statement on Thursday after various countries raised concerns about Grok’s image-editing function that allowed users to digitally “undress” real people without their consent.
“This issue has raised concerns in various jurisdictions. The PCPD is also concerned about the matter and is proactively contacting the relevant organisation to understand the situation,” a spokesman said.
The watchdog weighed in hours after Musk’s company xAI Corp, which developed the chatbot, announced that it had “implemented technological measures” to prevent the technology from undressing images of real people.
A spate of incidents starting late December drew global backlash after users of the AI chatbot, which is integrated into social media platform X, generated and circulated sexualised images of real people, including minors. X, formerly known as Twitter, was acquired by Musk in 2022.
The incidents led to swift reactions from various governments around the world, including the United Kingdom’s online safety regulator, which opened inquiries. Malaysia and Indonesia have temporarily restricted access to Grok, while European authorities signalled potential enforcement under digital safety rules.

The PCPD spokesman reminded members of the public on Thursday that when providing personal data to AI chatbots to generate AI content, they must comply with the requirements of the Personal Data (Privacy) Ordinance (PDPO) and relevant data protection principles.
The watchdog added that the use of AI chatbots to generate indecent or malicious photos and videos may violate the PDPO or even constitute criminal offences.
It urged residents not to share personal data of themselves or others with AI chatbots arbitrarily. Permission must first be obtained if using others’ information to generate AI content.
It also warned that people should never use chatbots to generate illegal content such as child pornography.

data.title: Asean urged to rethink stance as Myanmar election risks normalising junta rule
data.description:
With Myanmar in the throes of an election widely expected to return its military junta to power, calls have renewed for Asean to rethink its approach of not engaging the regime.
The region, observers warn, should also brace itself for the “very real possibility” that half of Southeast Asia might eventually recognise the results of Myanmar’s election and by extension the ruling junta.
On Sunday, Myanmar began its second round of voting in the country’s first general election since the military usurped power in 2021 from the legitimate government headed by Nobel laureate Aung San Suu Kyi.
Voting expanded to additional townships including some areas affected by the civil war between the government and its armed opponents.
The country’s main pro-military party on Monday claimed victory in the parliamentary seat of Suu Kyi, with the junta declaring that the polls would return power to the people.
An official from the Union Solidarity and Development Party (USDP) said they “won in Kawhmu”, Suu Kyi’s former seat in Yangon region.

The month-long election – with the final phase scheduled for January 25 – has been widely derided by regional and international observers as a ploy to prolong junta rule.
Many also said the polls had been rigged by a crackdown on dissent and a ballot stacked with military allies.
Hunter Marston, an adjunct fellow at the Centre for Strategic and International Studies’ Southeast Asia programme, said the Association of Southeast Asian Nations should rethink its approach to the Five-Point Consensus by pressuring the junta to directly engage with the resistance.
The Consensus is a diplomatic peace plan adopted by Asean in April 2021 to address the political crisis and violence in Myanmar following the military coup. It calls for the cessation of violence and constructive dialogue among all parties.
“Asean needs to look beyond the Consensus. It should encourage Indonesia’s approach of holding private meetings with junta representatives and members of the opposition with the goal of bringing the two sides into direct political negotiations,” Marston said.
“This is the only way to end the conflict.”
Asean’s special envoy for Myanmar, he said, “got off to a terrible start” by meeting junta leader Min Aung Hlaing and “falling into a classic PR trap laid out by the Myanmar military”.
Philippine Secretary of Foreign Affairs and Special Envoy for Myanmar Theresa Lazaro had visited Myanmar on January 6, marking the beginning of her country’s Asean chairmanship this year.

Marston said Asean needed to “reverse the impression that it has taken a soft approach to the junta and been feckless in its attempts to implement the Consensus”.
The bloc should meet all stakeholders, including representatives of ethnic armed groups and the National Unity Government (NUG), “rather than showing the military undue deference”, he added.
Indonesia was said to have engaged extensively and quietly behind closed doors with key Myanmar stakeholders, including ethnic armies, and the NUG which comprised parliamentarians ousted during the coup, and its allies.
Marston said the region should brace itself for the “very real possibility” that half the Asean states would recognise the results of Myanmar’s election and “normalise” the USDP as representative of the people’s will.
“Cambodia, Laos, Thailand and Vietnam are likely to fall into this camp, but Singapore and even Malaysia or the Philippines could conceivably come around to such a position. This would render the military’s power grab a fait accompli,” he said.
While the international community appeared to have no policy for such a scenario, “it had better consider one”, he added.
Thailand, Vietnam and Cambodia have begun re-engaging the junta through trade, energy and even military cooperation, either bilaterally or through regional mechanisms such as the Lancang-Mekong cooperation.
Vietnam and Cambodia were also among those that sent delegations in the first phase of the election, according to Myanmar’s Ministry of Information.

Sharon Seah, senior fellow at the ISEAS – Yusof Ishak Institute, said these Southeast Asian countries were likely to implicitly recognise the new Myanmar government.
“This leaves Asean with a difficult choice: continue to marginalise the [junta], but to what end? Or to give implicit recognition and find a way to work with them?” she asked.
Asean had maintained that the Consensus remained its reference point, and Seah said the bloc should ask the “new government” to stop all forms of violence against the civilian population in exchange for a return to the Asean table.
“The Myanmar military is part of the problem so it must necessarily be part of the solution,” Seah said.
“Inasmuch as Asean may loathe the military government’s actions, the costs of non-engagement are being borne by the ordinary people of Myanmar.”
The 2021 coup has led to a catastrophic and wide-ranging humanitarian and human rights crisis, marked by mass displacement, economic collapse, brutal violence against civilians and widespread food insecurity and poverty.
The junta’s actions, according to rights groups, have amounted to war crimes and crimes against humanity, with more than 7,000 people killed and over 30,000 arrested, including civilians and activists.

Seah said Asean had always maintained that elections should be held only after the violence against the Myanmar people ended.
“[The military] ignored Asean and [went] ahead because they have support from their immediate neighbours,” Seah said, noting that these included China and India.
Beijing had “never stopped” engaging with the junta since 2021 and might give recognition to the new government, Seah noted, whereas New Delhi remained ambiguous in its response to the poll.
“[India has] neither objected nor supported, but eventually Indian pragmatism will prevail and it will not make a strong statement,” Seah said.
The armed forces have ruled Myanmar for most of the country’s post-independence history before a decade-long democratic thaw saw civilians assume control.
While Asean member states were unlikely to offer explicit recognition such as sending congratulatory letters, Seah said the accreditation of new ambassadors was expected to resume.

data.title: Rugby and racing join forces to make Hong Kong’s sevens week bigger and better
data.description:
An expanded Hong Kong 10s tournament and midweek racing at Happy Valley are among the changes being made to this year’s sevens week in the city, as the landmark tournament celebrates its 50th birthday in April.
For the first time, a partnership between Hong Kong China Rugby, the Hong Kong Jockey Club and Hong Kong Football Club will bring an element of cohesion to what is traditionally the biggest week in the city’s sporting calendar.
Rugby and racing will work in tandem through the week, with the announcement of the Hong Kong men’s and women’s teams planned for Sha Tin on Sunday, April 12, and the Wednesday race meeting expected to feature a sevens theme.
“We are delighted to be able to expand our long-running and successful collaboration with the Hong Kong Jockey Club and HKFC,” Chris Brooke, the HKRC chairman, said.
“Our organisations share a similar vision for the role that sport can play in our community. With the support of the Hong Kong Jockey Club, we have been able to share the experience and values of rugby with literally thousands of people from all over Hong Kong and within the Greater Bay Area.

“We look forward to growing these numbers together with the Hong Kong Jockey Club and HKFC even further in the future.”
The 40th edition of the 10s, which attracts teams from around the world, will be over three days – April 14 to 16 – and not the normal two at Football Club’s base in Happy Valley, where the horse racing will also take place, rather than being consigned to Sha Tin.
Neil Jensen, the Football Club chairman, said the partnership would allow the tournament to develop further and ensure “that the fan experience is enhanced and that we continue to attract the best players”.
The Jockey Club, meanwhile, will continue its role as the sevens’ official community partner. Last year, the HKJC took more than 6,500 people to the first day of the tournament at Kai Tak Stadium, while 1,500 members of its mini-rugby programme played at the ground before the main matches took place on Friday and Saturday.
“The rugby sevens and horse racing are two of Hong Kong’s most iconic sporting attractions, each drawing thousands of visitors to the city every year. It’s fantastic that we have been able to bring these traditions together through the Racing with Rugby Week,” Winfried Engelbrecht-Bresges, the Jockey Club’s CEO, said.
“The Jockey Club has long been a committed supporter of the city’s sports development and the rugby sevens community outreach programme. This partnership not only strengthens that commitment to community support but also reinforces Hong Kong’s status as a world-class sports tourism destination, creating a truly unique experience for local fans and visitors alike.”

data.title: 7 popular Chinese horse idioms for you to trot out in the Year of the Horse
data.description:
Horses played crucial roles in ancient China – in the military, transport and agriculture. People of different social classes interacted with and observed the animals, drawing inspiration for the arts, literature and mythology.
Over time, the horse became the subject of many idioms. Here are seven of the most popular horse-related expressions in Chinese, how to pronounce them in Cantonese and the stories behind them.
龍馬精神 Lung ma jing sun
Meaning “the spirit of dragons and horses”, this idiom is often used to wish someone well and bless them with vigour and vitality.
The dragon and the horse are both spirited and powerful animals. However, the “dragon” and “horse” in this context can also refer to longma, a lesser-known legendary creature with traits of both, known for its might and ability to tread on water.
The expression, which dates back to the Tang dynasty (618-907), is most commonly used during holidays, especially Lunar New Year, to wish senior relatives good health and strength.
萬馬奔騰 Maan ma bun tang
This idiom roughly translates as “10,000 stallions galloping”, evoking a magnificent, thundering sound.
There are differing accounts of its origins, though the Ming dynasty writer Ling Mengchu (1580-1644) is believed to be one of the earliest to use it.
Today, it generally denotes a formidable, unstoppable force, from trends and movements to loud phenomena such as firecracker explosions or roaring waterfalls.

指鹿為馬 Ji luk wai ma
Meaning “point at a deer and call it a horse”, the expression dates back to the Qin dynasty and the historical Chinese text Shiji (circa 91BC), and describes someone who deliberately distorts the truth for self-gain.
Zhao Gao (258BC-207BC) was a cunning politician who sought to seize power and eliminate his enemies after Emperor Qin Shi Huang died in 210BC.
One day, he rode a deer while accompanying the new emperor, Qin Er Shi (229BC-207BC), on an outing. When the emperor asked why Zhao was riding a deer, he replied, “It is a steed. If your Majesty doubts my words, you may ask the others.”
Half of the ministers said it was a deer, while the rest insisted it was a horse. The emperor chose to believe Zhao over what he saw with his own eyes.

走馬看花 Jau ma hon fa
Originally coined to describe the joy of admiring flowers at leisure while riding a horse, the phrase “flower viewing from horseback” has evolved to mean looking at something hastily and failing to grasp it fully.
It comes from a poem by Tang dynasty writer Meng Jiao (751-814), in which he described his profound joy after passing the final imperial examination and becoming a certified scholar at the age of 46.
Today, the expression is more commonly used to mean “galloping past flowers”, a hurried observation that does not lead to real understanding.
老馬識途 Lo ma sik to
Like veteran taxi drivers in Hong Kong, “an old horse knows the way”. This phrase is used to complement experienced individuals by saying they will always know better.
It dates back to the Zhou dynasty (1046BC-256BC), when three politicians led a campaign against a neighbouring state that began in spring.
When they returned, it was already winter and the landscape had changed significantly, causing them to lose their way home.
One of the three, Guan Zhong, remembered that old steeds could recall paths they had travelled, so he chose a few seasoned horses to lead the way.
They successfully retraced the route, giving rise to the idiom, which describes how experience helps people achieve better results.
騎牛搵馬 Ke ngau wan ma
In Hong Kong, “riding a cow to look for a horse” – settling for something less while seeking something better – is a popular phrase both in dating and at the workplace.
The idiom’s origin is unclear, but in the past, the saying was pejorative and implied a lack of loyalty. These days, “shopping around” is more socially acceptable, especially in the workplace, though less so in the dating scene.
懸崖勒馬 Yuen ngaai laak ma

One of the earliest records of the idiom “rein in the horse at the edge of the precipice” appears in a zaju, a form of Chinese opera, by Zheng Guangzu (1260-1320) of the Yuan dynasty.
Zheng wrote of an alleged exchange between King Xuan of Qi (circa 319BC-301BC) and his consort Zhongli Chun.
Although Zhongli is described in various historic texts as “exceptionally ugly”, she is also portrayed as wise and courageous.
On one occasion, she discussed the kingdom’s defence and military affairs with the king, advising that it was never too late to “rein in the horse at the edge of the precipice”, likening it to patching a leak when a boat is already midstream.
Today, the phrase is used to describe someone who is on the brink of danger but comes to their senses and turns back in time.

data.title: Bid-rigging clamp, smoke-free construction sites: Hong Kong’s Legco gets cracking
data.description:
Hong Kong’s new Legislative Council held its first meeting on Wednesday, with lawmakers focusing on follow-up work after last November’s deadly fire at Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po, which claimed the lives of 161 people and displaced nearly 5,000 residents.
Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu and government officials outlined priorities including rehousing the displaced residents, reforming industry practices and holding those responsible for the tragedy accountable.
The Post looks into the measures proposed by the government in response to the Wang Fuk Court fire.
1. Crackdown on bid-rigging
Public concern about bid-rigging and malpractices has grown in the wake of the Tai Po fire.
The Urban Renewal Authority (URA) introduced its “Smart Tender” platform in 2016 after a number of complaints over bid-rigging.
While it is designed to provide technical support to homeowners in procuring consultants and contractors, the platform cannot prevent bid-rigging syndicates from entering the market.
Prestige Construction & Engineering, the company currently under investigation by the city’s corruption-busting body, also used the Smart Tender service.

Chief Secretary Eric Chan Kwok-ki said the government would optimise the existing platform, providing a preselected list of contractors for homeowners.
He said authorities would regularly inspect renovation projects receiving government subsidies with major phases or changes, including any significant increase in construction fees, reported to them.
The URA would also carry out post-renovation surveys to gauge residents’ satisfaction, which would in turn affect contractors’ eligibility to bid for future projects.
Secretary for Development Bernadette Linn Hon-ho proposed expanding the URA’s remit to conduct tendering and bid evaluation for property owners, in addition to providing advice through the tender platform.
“If the building is applying for government maintenance subsidies, the owners must accept the consultant and contractor decided by the URA after its bid evaluation as a condition,” she said.
Linn added that contractors and consultants awarded bids involving government subsidies must report key progress milestones to the authority.
For projects not receiving subsidies, the URA would still conduct tendering and bid evaluation, advising owners on suitable contractors and consultants to avoid predatory practices.
Authorities aim to table relevant legislative bills in the second half of the year.
2. Higher voting bar for property owners
The renovation cost for Wang Fuk Court, which ballooned from an initial HK$152 million to HK$330 million, was approved at an owners’ corporation meeting.
Proxy votes have been widely reported as a tactic used by some people to secure approval for large renovation projects.
Secretary for Home and Youth Affairs Alice Mak Mei-kuen said reforms were needed to prevent fraud and improve decision-making in owners’ corporations, particularly for costly building maintenance projects.
Her bureau has proposed raising the attendance and voting thresholds for approving large-scale maintenance works and high-value procurements, making it harder for small groups to push through major projects.
To boost transparency and reduce manipulation risks, a new cap would be introduced on the number of proxy votes one person can hold.
A public notice would also disclose which flats have assigned their voting rights to a proxy.

3. Tighter oversight on building repairs
Observers said the fire exposed systemic government oversight failures, including the undetected use of substandard scaffolding nets and flammable foam boards.
Linn proposed major changes to improve safety and oversight of large-scale building repair projects.
Under the plan, major maintenance works would be reclassified from “Grade 2 minor works” to “Grade 1 minor works,” subjecting them to stricter regulations.
“This means that in the future, all large-scale building maintenance projects will require not only the hiring of a project contractor but also a third-party professional,” Linn said.
The main change is a new requirement to appoint an independent third-party professional to design and oversee the construction and safety plans, acting as an owners’ watchdog to ensure contractors perform the work properly and safely.
She said that would give the Buildings Department a clearer basis for supervision and intervention.
4. Construction sites to go smoke-free
While the cause of the Tai Po fire has not yet been identified, some residents had previously complained about workers smoking at the construction site.
Secretary for Labour and Welfare Chris Sun Yuk-han announced plans to tighten health and safety measures in the construction industry, including a blanket ban on smoking at all construction sites.
He said that while current law allowed smoking bans on a case-by-case basis where flammable materials were present, the proposed legislation would make all construction sites completely smoke-free.
The Labour Department has begun amending the Construction Sites Safety Regulations to impose clear legal responsibilities on main contractors, subcontractors and all on-site personnel, prohibiting smoking to reduce fire risks.
“Our goal is to submit the relevant amendments to the Legco for consideration in the coming weeks,” said Sun.
Additional reporting by Leopold Chen

data.title: Drink in focus: Egg Waffle cocktail at Avoca
data.description:
Few snacks are as ubiquitous to our city as egg waffles, and if any bar were to reimagine classic Hong Kong snacks as cocktails, few are more appropriate than Avoca of Mondrian Hong Kong, situated in the heart of Tsim Sha Tsui.
First seen in the 1950s, the egg waffle is comprised of eggs, flour, sugar and evaporated milk cooked in a waffle iron and best enjoyed fresh, when the snack is crispy on the outside and fluffy on the inside. It’s a popular streetside snack and comes in a variety of flavours like matcha, red bean, chocolate – even salted egg yolk.
The Egg Waffle cocktail is one of a series of cocktails on Avoca’s current menu that are inspired by staple Hong Kong flavours like those of the yuenyeung coffee and tea hybrid drink, cured sausage or lap cheong, and more.

“[The egg waffle is] a nostalgic quintessential Hong Kong treat and has been my favourite afternoon indulgence since childhood,” says Avoca head bartender Rolf Flaminiano. “We wanted to capture that comforting, familiar flavour in a drink that surprises and evokes wonderful memories for Hongkongers, and [offers] a delicious way to experience the city for visitors.”
Flaminiano and the team begin this cocktail by infusing Michter’s rye whiskey with egg waffles made in-house, which are then both blended with vanilla and sous-vided to extract as much flavour as possible. The result is then shaken with banana oleo, chocolate bitters, citric acid and egg yolk liqueur. (Banana oleo saccharum is a syrup made by macerating banana peels in sugar syrup.)

“This creates a rich, layered profile that balances sweetness, tartness and a warm whiskey punch,” says Flaminiano, “Banana oleo adds depth and aroma while supporting our sustainability ethos by repurposing banana peels into syrup instead of discarding them. The egg yolk liqueur brings a custard-like creaminess and nostalgic character, offering a more refined sweetness and longer shelf life than fresh yolks.”
For the latter, Flaminiano prefers Advocaat, an egg yolk liqueur from the Netherlands that adds brandy to the equation and “has a thick, custard-like texture ... quite similar to eggnog but richer”.
“The Egg Waffle has become one of our top sellers at Avoca,” he concludes. “It’s a drink that tells a story … and our guests love its uniqueness and minimalist presentation. They’re intrigued by this new way to reimagine a legendary treat.”
Anatomy of a cocktail
Name: Egg Waffle
Bar: Avoca, 38/F, Mondrian Hong Kong, 8A Hart Avenue, Tsim Sha Tsui
Price: HK$138
Main ingredients: Egg waffle-infused Michter’s rye whiskey, banana oleo saccharum, Advocaat egg yolk liqueur, chocolate bitters, citric acid
Presentation: Served in a cocktail glass with a single egg waffle piece as garnish
Flavours: Rich, sweet, custard-like, vanilla, fruit notes

data.title: Meet Shawn Mendes’ rumoured girlfriend, Brazilian model and actress Bruna Marquezine
data.description:
Shawn Mendes is seemingly starting the new year hand in hand with a new bae. The “Stitches” singer, 27, who is rumoured to be dating Brazilian model and actress Bruna Marquezine, was spotted enjoying a hiking date with the 30-year-old in Los Angeles over the weekend, reports Just Jared.
The pair haven’t tried to hide their apparent new romance since being linked in December. They enjoyed New Year’s celebrations at Alagoas, per Hola! magazine, and were seen out and about in LA, fetching groceries together just last week.

Mendes, who previously dated Camila Cabello and sparked romance rumours with Sabrina Carpenter, told fans during one of his For Friends and Family Tour concerts that he was “figuring out” his sexuality. Meanwhile, according to the Daily Mail, Marquezine once dated football star Neymar.
So, what else do we know about Bruna Marquezine?
She was a child actress

Marquezine hails from Duque de Caxias, Rio de Janeiro, where she was born to Neide Maia and Telmo Reis. She started acting at a young age, attending auditions with her mother, per Flaunt magazine. At five years old, she was already doing commercials. It didn’t take her long to transition to TV roles. By age seven, Marquezine was appearing in soap operas. A year later, she made her silver screen debut with Xuxa Abracadabra (2003).
“I know it’s kind of weird to think that a five-year-old would know that she wanted to act, but that’s how I felt at the time. I loved that I had responsibilities and would often be treated as a professional, as an adult,” Marquezine recalled to Elle.
Where have you seen her before?

Marquezine is best known for telenovelas like Mulheres Apaixonadas, Negócio da China and Cobras e Lagartos. She ventured into Hollywood by trying out for the role of Supergirl in DC’s The Flash, per Flaunt. While she did not get the part, she was later asked to audition for Blue Beetle (2023) and was cast as Jenny Kord. “I’ve always wanted my work to allow me to work outside of my country,” she told Elle, adding, “I hope that for my work, and for my art, there are no limits.”
She has other talents, too

Besides acting, Marquezine is also a director and producer. She co-directed and executive-produced the series Benefits with Friends, in which she also appears.
She is also a dancer and participated in the 10th season of the reality show Dança dos Famosos – a sort of Brazilian Dancing with the Stars – where she was a runner-up.
Last but not least, she is also a model and walked for Dolce & Gabbana at Milan Fashion Week in 2018. Additionally, she has collaborated with brands like Chopard, Intimissimi, Puma and Tiffany & Co.
